skill.name: using-superpowers
skill.description: Meta-skill that instructs an assistant to check for relevant skills before responding and to follow skill workflows when they apply.
skill.summary: Enforces a skill-first workflow for assistants that support reusable skills.
skill.author: CodingCossack

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography findings were false positives: SKILL.md contains a Graphviz diagram and workflow text, not executable shell code or cryptographic logic. The skill does contain a prompt-injection style attempt to impose non-negotiable behavior before any response, so it should be blocked pending human review.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: The skill tells the assistant that it must check for skills before any response and that it does not have a choice if a skill applies. This attempts to impose global behavior across conversations and can conflict with higher-priority user or platform instructions.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The quoted lines explicitly use mandatory language and apply it before any response. This is a clear semantic prompt-control risk even though it is not malware.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: The skill states that user instructions do not grant permission to skip workflows. This can pressure the assistant to prioritize the skill's process over the user's requested execution style.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The language directly limits how user instructions should be interpreted. It is risky in a marketplace skill because the behavior applies broadly beyond a narrow task domain.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Finding: Ruby Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged line 22 as backtick execution, but the backticks start a fenced dot code block for a Graphviz workflow diagram. No executable shell command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is a markdown code fence labeled dot, and the contents define graph nodes and edges. There is no Ruby code path or shell invocation.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Finding: Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged lines 3, 20, 67, and 82 as weak cryptography, but those lines contain workflow descriptions and skill-priority text. No cryptographic algorithm or implementation appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found only natural-language workflow instructions at each flagged line. There is no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or related crypto usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Mandatory Prompt-Level Workflow Control
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses non-negotiable language to require skill checks before any response and to require following applicable skills. This is dangerous when installed from a community source because it can override normal assistant judgment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and central to the skill. It does not require inference from hidden code or external files.

content.value_statement: AI assistants can skip relevant skills when tasks seem simple. This skill provides a structured process for checking, selecting, and following skills before work begins.
actual_capabilities: Defines when an assistant should check for applicable skills.
Provides a Graphviz flow for deciding whether to invoke a skill.
Instructs the assistant to announce skill usage and purpose.
Directs checklist-based skills to become task items.
Lists common rationalizations that lead to skipped workflows.
Defines priority between process skills and implementation skills.
limitations: It does not implement a tool, script, or automated validation step.
It can be too broad for assistants that require strict instruction hierarchy.
It depends on the host assistant having skill discovery support.
It does not define safeguards for conflicting user or platform instructions.

use_cases.title: Standardize Assistant Workflows
use_cases.description: Require consistent skill checks before coding, writing, or analysis tasks.

use_cases.title: Reduce Missed Process Skills
use_cases.description: Help assistants remember debugging, testing, or planning skills before execution.

use_cases.title: Train Skill-Based Agents
use_cases.description: Teach agents to select process skills before implementation skills.

prompt_templates.title: Start With Skill Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill-first workflow for this task. Check whether any available skill applies before answering.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Skill Priority
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the available skills for this task. Choose process skills before implementation skills and explain the order briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Create Skill Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: If the selected skill has a checklist, convert the checklist into task items and follow them while completing the work.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Workflow Compliance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this assistant interaction for missed skill checks, skipped workflows, and unsupported rationalizations. Provide concrete corrections.

output_examples.input: Ask the assistant to fix a bug with skills available.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies that a debugging workflow applies, announces it, creates checklist tasks, and then investigates the bug.

output_examples.input: Ask the assistant to build a feature with process and implementation skills available.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a planning or brainstorming skill first, then applies the implementation skill after the approach is clear.

output_examples.input: Ask for a quick answer when a relevant skill may apply.
output_examples.output: The assistant performs the skill check before answering and explains whether a skill was used.

best_practices: Use this skill only in environments with clear instruction hierarchy controls.
Pair it with narrow domain skills that have explicit scopes and checklists.
Review conflicts between this workflow and user instructions before publishing.
anti_patterns: Do not use it to override platform, safety, or security instructions.
Do not install it as a hidden global behavior for all users.
Do not treat the workflow as a substitute for human review.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It tells an assistant to check for relevant skills before responding and to follow applicable skill workflows.

faq.question: Does it run code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed file contains markdown instructions and a Graphviz diagram, not executable code.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The skill uses mandatory language that can control assistant behavior across conversations and conflict with higher-priority instructions.

faq.question: Can it be used safely?
faq.answer: It should not be published until its scope is narrowed and safeguards for instruction hierarchy are added.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is the static command-execution alert confirmed?
faq.answer: No. The flagged location is a markdown code fence for a diagram, not a shell command.
